behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the backoff calculation itself (attempt count -> delay with cap), which is local logic with no required module handoff or I/O. Rejected Integration because behavior text is only about delay growth/cap, not cross-module state propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure/backoff-policy function (or single retry-policy object) with consecutive failure counts and a max cap, asserting delays like 100, 200, 400... then capped at configured max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at a module boundary: response classification + retry/backoff policy wiring. A unit test of only classifier or only backoff can miss mismatched wiring. Rejected System because behavior does not require real HTTP/network; synthetic HTTP response objects are sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator with a stub transport returning a 429 (or provider-specific quota error payload) and verify it is treated as retriable and produces the expected computed delay.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Needs interaction of timer/scheduler + retry orchestrator + request transport to prove resend of the same request and transparent return path. Rejected System because behavior text does not require real network timing; fake timers and stub transport expose this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock plus stub transport: first call returns rate-limit, second call returns success. Assert one scheduled wait, second attempt payload matches original request, and `call()` resolves to the successful result without extra caller handling.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Requires retry-limit enforcement wired to final error mapping/type emission. A unit test of the error class alone would miss boundary mistakes where orchestrator throws generic errors. Rejected System because no real OS/network behavior is needed to detect wrong final error type.
behaviors.test_description: Configure small max retries; stub transport always returns rate-limit. Assert `call()` rejects with `QuotaExhaustedError` (type/instance and key fields) and not a generic error after the final allowed attempt.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly spans multiple local components (retry chain + quota tracking + termination condition), so module interaction is the failure boundary. Rejected System because infinite-loop/termination bugs here can be reproduced with in-process stubs and fake timers; no real infrastructure required.
behaviors.test_description: Run orchestrator with fake timers and deterministic failing transport; assert total attempts equals configured initial+retries, terminal error is surfaced, and no further timers/attempts remain scheduled.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is shared-state coordination across concurrent callers and quota bucket module; single-function unit tests miss cross-caller interaction. Rejected System because behavior text scopes to "same process" and does not require real network/OS primitives beyond in-process async scheduling.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/shared quota manager; fire concurrent `call()` invocations via Promise.all with controlled stub transport. Assert aggregate consumed tokens/permits reflect one shared bucket, not per-caller independent buckets.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering bugs arise in queue + dispatcher interaction under async contention; this is beyond a single local function in most implementations. Rejected System because FIFO dispatch order can be validated with in-process queueing and controlled stubs without real HTTP.
behaviors.test_description: Queue multiple blocked requests in known submission order with a controlled scheduler/stub transport, release capacity, and assert dispatch/start order matches submission order exactly.
